Abstract
Atthe centerof the Schoolof Athens, Raphaelpainted Platowith aface similar
tothat of Leonardoda Vinci. Inthis article Iarguethat thelikeness wasintentional, andthat RaphaelвҖҷsfrescocontains asetof referencesto thebook De
Divina Proportione, towhich Leonardocontributed withdrawings ofpolyhedrons. De Divina Proportionewas writtenby LeonardoвҖҷsfriendand teacher of
mathematics, Luca Pacioli, andcontains argumentsfor raisingthe statusof the
artof painting, similarto thearguments foundin LeonardoвҖҷs Paragone. Pacioli
and Leonardothought paintingshould beregarded asaliberal art, dueto the
paintersвҖҷ useof mathematicalprinciples. Inthis article, Ishowhow Plato with
theface of Leonardois partof asetof allusionsto thesearguments foundin The
Schoolof Athens.
Keywords: Raphael, Leonardoda Vinci, Luca Pacioli, The Schoolof Athens,
Plato, Timaeus, quadrivium

Vasariwrote about RaphaelвҖҷsadmirationfor Leonardoin his Livesof the Artists:
[...] when [Raphael] sawthe worksof Leonardoda Vinci, whohad no equal
inrendering expressionsin theheads ofmen aswell aswomen, andwho in
givinggrace tohis figuresand theirmovements surpassedall otherpainters,
Raphaelwas leftwholly astonishedand amazed; inshort, sincethe style of
Leonardopleased himmore thanany otherhe hadever seen, heset himself
tostudying it, andgradually leavingbehind PietroвҖҷsstylealbeit with great
effort, hesought toimitate thestyle of Leonardoto thebest ofhis knowledge
andability [пңөпң¶, page пңіпңіпңұ]. Seealso [пң¶пң°] and [пңұпңІ]. Leonardoda Vinciin RaphaelвҖҷs Schoolof Athens Bramanteworked togetherwith Leonardoat thecourt in Milanfor almost
twentyyears, andwas employedby Pope Julius II in Romewhen Raphael
paintedhis Schoolof Athensin the Vatican Palace.пңі

fromaround пңұпңөпңұпңІ, whilethe drawingto theright isasomewhat idealized

RaphaelвҖҷsfrescobelongs toaset ofreferences foundin The Schoolof Athens,
connectingthe frescowith themathematician Luca PacioliвҖҷsbook De Divina

ofmathematics, and Leonardocontributed to De Divina Proportione with
drawingsof geometricsolids. Themanuscript of De Divina Proportione was

finishedin пңұпңҙпң№пңё, andthree manuscriptcopies weremade. Thefirst printed
versionwas publishedin Venicein пңұпңөпң°пң№, whichcoincides withwhen Raphael
begunhis workon The Schoolof Athens.
Inthe secondand thirdparts ofthe article, Iwillargue, basedon aspects
of RaphaelвҖҷsfresco, thatthe referencesto PacioliвҖҷs De Divina Proportione
shouldbe interpretedin lightof Leonardoda VinciвҖҷsambitionof establishing
paintingas asciencebased onmathematics, elevatingthe statusof painting
fromthat ofamere craft. LeonardoвҖҷsthoughtson thisissue hadnot been
publishedwhen Raphaelpainted The Schoolof Athens, butin De Divina
Proportione Luca Paciolipresents argumentsfor raisingthe statusof painting, similarto thoseof Leonardo. Thetheory developedin thisarticle is
thatthe referencesto Leonardoda Vinciand to PacioliвҖҷs De Divina Proportionefound in The Schoolof Athensform partof amessagetelling us that
thepainter isan intellectualon aparwith thephilosophers, scientists, and
mathematiciansdepicted in RaphaelвҖҷsfresco.
пңұ. Luca Pacioli, Plato, and Euclid
Iwantto beginby takingacloser lookat afamousportrait of Luca Pacioli
(Figure пңі) sinceit presentsus withmany ofthe featureswhich latershow up
inhis De Divina Proportione, inparticular afocuson the so-called Platonic
solidsand on Euclid, butalso becausethe paintingonce hungon awall in
the Ducal Palacein RaphaelвҖҷshometownof Urbino.пңҙ Raphael frequented
thecourt of Urbino, wherehis fatherhad beenacourt painter, and Raphael
himselfpainted portraitsof severalmen andwomen attachedto thecourt.
Sincethe portraitof Pacioli, commonlyattributed to Jacopo deвҖҷ Barbari,
withits focuson mathematics, containsseveral ofthe elementswe shall
seeconnects The Schoolof Athenswith PacioliвҖҷs De Divina Proportione,
Paciolihad tutored Guidobaldoda Montefeltro (the Dukeof Urbinofrom пңұпңҙпңёпңІ to
пңұпңөпң°пңё), inmathematics inhis youth. Theportrait waspainted soonafter Pacioli published
his Summade arithmeticain пңұпңҙпң№пңҙ, whichwas dedicatedto Guidobaldoand isalso depicted
inthe painting. The inscriptionвҖңDivo Principi GuidoвҖқ earlierfound alongthe bottom of
theportrait hasbeen interpretedas eitherstating thatthe portraitwas dedicated to
Guidobaldoor that Guidobaldois theyoung manstanding tothe rightin thepainting.
Aninventory documentsthat thepainting wasin the Ducal Palacein пңұпң¶пңіпңұ [пңө].
пңҙ Leonardoda Vinciin RaphaelвҖҷs Schoolof Athens itcould possiblyhave beenan inspirationfor Raphael.пңө

Figure пңі: Jacopo deвҖҷ Barbari, Portraitof Luca Pacioli, пңұпңҙпң№пңө. Publicdomain image from
Wikipedia (https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Pacioli.jpg), last accessed
on July пңі, пңІпң°пңІпңұ. Theman dressedin friarrobes, pointingat ageometricconstruction is Luca
Pacioli (Figure пңі). Aregulardodecahedron, consistingof twelveregular pentagons, isplaced onthe bookwith redcovers tothe right (PacioliвҖҷsown
Summade arithmetica). Thisis oneof thefive Platonicsolids, calledвҖңPlatonicвҖқ becausethey weredescribed by Platoin his Timaeus. Onthe frame
ofthe tabletto theleft wecan read EVCLIDES. Theopen book Pacioli has
placedhis lefthand onis EuclidвҖҷsmainmathematical work, The Elements.
Atthe timewhen Raphaelbegun hiswork on The Schoolof Athens, in пңұпңөпң°пң№ or пңұпңөпңұпң°,
Luca Paciolihad madeaname forhimself byfirst holdingapublic lectureabout EuclidвҖҷs
Elementsin Venicein пңұпңөпң°пңё, where пңөпң°пң° peopleattended. Thefollowing year (пңұпңөпң°пң№) both
De Divina Proportioneand a PacioliвҖҷs Latinedition of EuclidвҖҷs Elementswere published.
пңө Frode Sirnes Larsen

Studyingthe paintingmore closelythan whatthe resolutionof the image
aboveprovides for, onecan seethat Pacioliis pointingto aproposition from
book XIII of The Elements [пңө]. Thispresents uswith onemore connection to
the Platonicsolids, sincebook XIII of The Elementsis where Euclid proves
thegeometric constructionsof the Platonicsolids.пң¶ Theportrait is assumed
tohave beenpainted in пңұпңҙпң№пңө, justbefore Pacioliwent to Milan, where he
collaboratedwith Leonardo Da Vincifrom пңұпңҙпң№пң¶ to пңұпңҙпң№пң№ [пңұпңё]. Itwas in Milan
De Divina Proportionewas completedin пңұпңҙпң№пңё, withdrawings by Leonardo.
Thetitle of De Divina Proportionerefers toits treatmentof whatis also
calledthe goldensection orgolden ratio.пң· Publishedin Venicein пңұпңөпң°пң№, the
bookconsists ofthree separatemanuscripts, ofwhich thefirst, Compendio
divinaproportione (Compendiumon the Divine Proportion) hasgiven the
bookits name. Themain focusof thismanuscript ison connections between
thegolden ratioand polyhedrons, inparticular theregular polyhedrons Plato
haddescribed inthe Timaeus. Themanuscript consistsof пң·пңІ shortchapters.
Inchapters пңұпң° to пңІпңІ, Paciolidescribes thirteenproperties ofthe divineproportion (called вҖҳeffectsвҖҷ (effecto)). Thefirst nineof theseproperties Pacioli
connectsto propositionsfrom book XIII ofthe Elements, which, as noted
above, isthe bookwhere Euclidtreats the Platonicsolids. Chapter пңІпңҙ to
пңөпңІ areexplicitly about PlatoвҖҷsregularsolids, withfrequent referencesto The
Elements, inparticular to Book XIII. Themanuscript alsocontains explicit
referencesto PlatoвҖҷstreatmentof theregular solidsin the Timaeus. We find
thisin chapter пңө, пңІпңІ, пңІпңҙ, andextensively inchapter пңөпңө.
Thethird manuscriptin thebook isan Italiantranslation of Piero della
FrancescaвҖҷstreatise Short Bookon the Five Regular Solids (Libellusde quinque
corporibus regularibus), which, asthe titlemakes clear, isalso aboutthe five
Platonicsolids. Atthe endof De Divina Proportionewe find пң¶пң° woodcuts
basedon Leonardoda VinciвҖҷsoriginaldrawings ofpolyhedrons, including ten
drawingsof thefive Platonicsolids, twoof each (Figure пңҙ).
Upto theleft inthe paintingwe seewhat Keplernamed arhombicuboctahedron,
halffilled withwater. Therhombicuboctahedron isdescribed inchapter пңөпңі of Divina
Proportione, andit isone ofthe polyhedronsdrawn by Leonardoin thebook.

Biblioteca Ambrosiana, availableat https://www.maa.org/book/export/html/116816,
lastaccessed on July пңі, пңІпң°пңІпңұ. Letus nowlook atsome ofthe correspondencesbetween The Schoolof Athens
and De Divina Proportione. Wehave seenthat the Platonicsolids described
in PlatoвҖҷs Timaeuswere amainfocus of PacioliвҖҷsbook, towhich Leonardo
contributeddrawings ofthe Platonicsolids. Atthe centerof The School of
Athens, wefind Platostanding nextto Aristotle. Raphaelchose bothto paint
Platowith theface of Leonardoand toplace the Timaeusin hisleft hand.
The Timaeuswas inthe renaissanceconsidered tobe oneof PlatoвҖҷsmostsignificant dialogues, andwe canimagine manydifferent reasonsfor placing that
particulardialogue in Plato/LeonardoвҖҷshand. In De Divina Proportione, the
treatmentof the Platonicsolids isclosely connectedto EuclidвҖҷs Elements. Frode Sirnes Larsen Ifthe faceof Leonardoand the Timaeusplaced in LeonardoвҖҷslefthand were
meantas allusionsto De Divina Proportione, weshould thereforeexpect a
pictorialconnection with Euclid, andthat iswhat wefind in The School of
Athens. Figure пңө: Detailfrom The Schoolof Athens (Figure пңұ). Euclidis depictedin theforeground tothe rightin RaphaelвҖҷsfresco, ashe is
explainingageometric constructionto agroupof pupils (Figure пңө). Comparing Platoand Euclid, wefind that Raphaelhas chosento dressthem in
almostidentical clothes. Both Platoand Euclidare wearingared himation
ontop ofatunic. Thehimation iscovering theirleft shouldersand not their
rightshoulders. Lookingat theback of Euclid, histunic isyellow, andtherefore ofadifferent colorfrom PlatoвҖҷstunic, butthe shadowon EuclidвҖҷsbreast
andright upperarm givesthose partsof EuclidвҖҷstunicacolor verysimilar to
thecolor of PlatoвҖҷstunic. Both Platoand EuclidвҖҷstunicscover their upper
rightarms, whiletheir rightforearms arebare. Andthey areboth painted
withthe indexfinger ontheir righthands stretchedout. Leonardoda Vinciin RaphaelвҖҷs Schoolof Athens

Dressing Euclidand Platoin similarclothes mayindicate that Raphael wants
usto seeaconnection betweenthem. Byplacing the Timaeusin PlatoвҖҷs
hand, Raphaelcould betelling usthat theconnection concernsthe Platonic
solidsdescribed bothin the Timaeusand in EuclidвҖҷs Elements. Andby giving
Platothe faceof Leonardo, Raphaelmight bereferring to PacioliвҖҷsbook with
itsfocus onthe Platonicsolids and EuclidвҖҷs Elements, where Leonardo drew
modelsof the Platonicsolids.
Strengtheningthe ideathat Raphaelin The Schoolof Athenshas intended
areferenceto De Divina Proportione, weshould noticethat thereis also a
correspondencebetween thedepiction of Plato/Leonardoin thefresco and
thebeginning of PacioliвҖҷsbook. Raphaelhas painted Leonardoin the center
ofthe frescoas heengages inapublic debatewith groupsof listenerson each
side (Figure пң¶). Figure пң¶: Detailfrom The Schoolof Athens (Figure пңұ). Correspondingto this, De Divina Proportionebegins bydescribing apublic
debatetaking placeat thecourt ofthe Dukeof Milanin Februaryof пңұпңҙпң№пңё,
atatime when Leonardowas employedby the Duke.
Today, Eminent Duke, the пң№thday of February, theyear of our
Lord пңұпңҙпң№пңё, weare gatheredin theimpregnable citadelof your Frode Sirnes Larsen

illustriouscity of Milan, themost worthyplace ofyour usual
residence, inthe presenceof yourmajesty, andan assembly [dedicated to] praiseworthyscientific debate, composedof people of
allranks, famousand mostwise, bothreligious andsecular, with
whomyour magnificentcourt constantlyabounds. [пңҙпңұ, page пңұ].
Paciolithen listsseveral ofthe participantsin thedebate, beforehe points
outthat oneof theparticipants was Leonardoda Vinci. Corresponding to
how Leonardois placedat thecenter ofthe debatein The Schoolof Athens,
Pacioliallots morespace tohis laudatorypresentation of Leonardothan to
theother participantsin thedebate takentogether.пңё As Monica Azzolini
notesin her articleвҖңAnatomyof a Dispute: Leonardo, Pacioliand Scientific
Courtly Entertainmentin Renaissance MilanвҖқ:
Remarkably, thepraise of LeonardoвҖҷsskillsas asculptor and
paintertakes upalmost twopages ofthe manuscript. No other
singlefigure receivesnearly thesame attentionas Leonardo. [пңҙ,
page пңұпңұпң№]
Toregard Platopainted withthe faceof Leonardo, the Timaeusplaced in
Plato/LeonardoвҖҷshand, thesimilar clothesof Platoand Euclid, and the
correspondencewith theopening of De Divina Proportioneas allusions to
PacioliвҖҷsbook, requiresan amountof interpretation. Inthe secondand third
partsof thisarticle Iwillpresent severalother featuresof The School of
Athenswhich similarlywill beinterpreted asreferring to Divina Proportione.
Butbefore Iturnto parttwo, Iwantto pointout that Stanzadella Segnatura,
theroom inthe Vaticanpalace where Raphaelpainted The Schoolof Athens
onone ofthe walls, containsone verydirect andunmistakable reference to
PacioliвҖҷs Divina Proportione.пң№
Itis worthnoticing thatthe courtpoet Bellincioni, inapoem publishedin his Rime
in пңұпңҙпң№пңі, calls Milanthe new Athens, andcompares Leonardoto therenowned classical
Greekpainter Apelles:вҖңMilanis thenew Athens! Here Lodovicoholds his Parnassus; here
rareand excellentartists flockas beesto seekhoney fromthe flowers; here, chief among
themall, isthe new Apelleswhom hehas broughtfrom Florence.вҖқ [пңұпңҙ, page пңұпңұпң№].

Iturnnow tothe secondpart ofthis article, where Iwillpresent apossible
explanationof why The Schoolof Athenscontains thesereferences to De

пңІ. The Quadrivium
Theeducated culturaland intellectualelites atthe courts, universities, and
cathedralchapters in Italyhad traditionallyregarded thepainter as amere
craftsmanwhose manuallabor requiredpractical skilland not intellectual
knowledge.пңұпңі From Leonardoda VinciвҖҷsnotebooks, weknow that Leonardo
waspreoccupied withraising thestatus ofpainting. Themost well-known
examplesof thispreoccupation arefound inthe manuscriptreferred toas the
Codex Urbinas Latinus пңұпңІпң·пң°. Themanuscript isthought tohave beencompiled from LeonardoвҖҷsnotebooksby hisassistant Francesco Melzi [пңҙпңі, page
пң№пңө]. Inthe firstpart ofthe manuscript, oftenreferred toas the Paragone,
Leonardoargues thatpainting isascience basedon principlesof mathematics, andthat ittherefore shouldbe classifiedas belongingto theliberal arts.пңұпңҙ
See [пңұпңё, chapter пңҙ]. Seealso KristellerвҖҷsinfluential articleвҖңThe Modern Systemof the
ArtsвҖқ, wherehe writes:
пңұпңі вҖңFromthe endof thefourteenth centurythrough thesixteenth, the writings
ofthe artistsand ofauthors sympatheticto thevisual artsrepeat the claim
thatpainting shouldbe consideredas oneof theliberal, notof themechanical arts. [...] theclaim of Renaissancewriters onpainting tohave their
artrecognized asliberal, howeverweakly supportedby classicalauthority,

пңҙ. Conclusion
Inthis article Ihaveargued that RaphaelвҖҷsportrayalof Platowith the face
of Leonardoshould beunderstood asalluding to LeonardoвҖҷsarguments that
paintingdeserves tobe countedas aliberalart dueto itsbasis inmathematics. LeonardoвҖҷsargumentshad notbeen publishedwhen Raphael painted
The Schoolof Athens, butaversion ofhis argumentsare foundin De Divina Proportione, abookwritten by LeonardoвҖҷsfriend, the mathematician
Luca Pacioli. Iarguethat placingthe Timaeusin Leonardo/PlatoвҖҷshand
servesseveral purposes. Italludes to PacioliвҖҷs De Divina Proportione, since
the Platonicsolids Leonardodrew for PacioliвҖҷsbookare describedin the
Timaeus. Basedon howthe disciplinesof thequadrivium are instantiated
inthe Timaeus, placingthat bookin LeonardoвҖҷshandalso alludesto PacioliвҖҷsversionof LeonardoвҖҷsarguments, withits focuson including painting
amongthe quadrivialarts. Raphaelseems toconfirm thatinterpretation by
locatingthe handholding the Timaeusat thevanishing pointof thelinear perspectivein thefresco, andcopying detailsfrom LeonardoвҖҷs The Last
Supper. Therepresentation ofthe quadrivialdisciplines, andhow that is
connectedto thelocation ofpainters inthe fresco, givesfurther support for
thisinterpretation.пңҙпңі
